subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you become a statistician – someone who asks good questions, collects data carefully, organises it, and reads the story hidden inside the numbers. Statistics is everywhere. When a shopkeeper in the Hargeisa market works out the most popular size of sambusa to prepare each morning, when a health worker in Garissa studies how many children were vaccinated this year, or when Yusuf compares the goals his favourite football team scored across a season, they are all doing statistics.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Tell the difference between a statistical question and a non-statistical question, and plan an investigation to answer one.
Classify data as categorical or numerical, and numerical data as discrete or continuous.
Choose a sensible way to collect data, understand population and sample, and recognise bias.
Organise raw data into frequency tables, including grouped and two-way tables.
Draw and read bar charts, pictograms, pie charts, line graphs and stem-and-leaf diagrams.
Calculate the three averages – mean, median and mode – and the range, and know when each average is most useful.
Interpret data, compare two sets of data, spot misleading graphs, and write valid conclusions.

concepts.title: Statistical Questions and the Investigation Cycle
concepts.explanation: Statistics begins with a good question. A statistical question is one that you answer by collecting data, and where you expect the answers to vary. Compare these two questions: “How old is Farah?” has one fixed answer – it is not statistical. But “How old are the students in Year 7 at our school?” expects a variety of answers, and you can only answer it by collecting data. That makes it a statistical question. Here are more examples so the difference becomes clear:
concepts.example: Statistical: “What is the most popular sport among Year 7 students?” (answers vary)

concepts.title: Types of Data
concepts.explanation: Before you collect or organise data, you must know what type of data it is, because the type decides which tables, charts and averages you can use. There are two big families: categorical and numerical. Categorical data (also called qualitative data) sorts things into groups or names. You cannot do sensible arithmetic with it. Examples include favourite colour, type of livestock (camel, goat, cattle), method of travel to school, and nationality. You can count how many fall into each group, but you cannot find an…
concepts.example: Numerical data (also called quantitative data) is made of numbers you can measure or count, and you *can* do arithmetic with it. Examples include age, height, number of siblings, and test scores. Numerical data splits…

concepts.title: Collecting Data – Samples and Bias
concepts.explanation: Imagine you want to know the favourite sport of every Year 7 student in a large city. Asking every single one would take far too long. Instead, statisticians study a sample – a smaller group chosen from the whole population – and use it to learn about everyone. The population is the entire group you are interested in; the sample is the part you actually collect data from. The most important idea in sampling is that a sample must be representative – it must fairly reflect the whole population. If your sample is…
concepts.example: Here are the main ways data is collected:

concepts.title: Organising Data with Frequency Tables
concepts.explanation: Raw data, straight from collection, is usually a messy list. A frequency table organises it so patterns become visible. Frequency simply means *how many times* a value appears. Often we build the table using a tally, marking one stroke for each item and crossing the fifth to make counting easy, then writing the total as the frequency. Here is a simple frequency table. Aisha asked 50 students at Nuur Academy their favourite school subject:
concepts.example: The percentage column shows the proportion in each group. To find it, divide the frequency by the total and multiply by 100. For Science: 15 ÷ 50 × 100 = 30%. The percentages always add up to 100%, which is a handy way…

concepts.title: Charts and Graphs
concepts.explanation: A good chart lets the eye see a pattern instantly. The skill is choosing the right chart for the type of data. Here are the ones you need in Year 7. Bar charts display categorical data using bars of equal width, with a gap between each bar because the categories are separate. The height of each bar shows its frequency. Always give the chart a title and label both axes. Bar charts are perfect for comparing groups, such as favourite sports or sales by product. A pictogram does the same job using pictures, where one…
concepts.example: Pie charts show how a whole is divided into parts. Each slice is a fraction of the full circle, and all the slices together make 360°. To find the angle for a slice, use: angle = (frequency ÷ total) × 360°. Pie charts…

concepts.title: Averages and Range
concepts.explanation: An average is a single value that stands for a whole set of data – a “typical” value. There are three different averages, and a good statistician knows all three and when to use each. There is also the range, which measures how spread out the data is. The mean is what most people call “the average.” To find it, add up all the values, then divide by how many values there are. Suppose a Kismaayo football team scored these goals in 9 matches: 2, 0, 3, 1, 2, 4, 2, 1, 3. The total is 2 + 0 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 3 =…
concepts.example: The median is the middle value once the data is placed in order. Using the ordered goals 0, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, there are 9 values, so the middle one is the 5th: the median is 2. If there is an even number of…

methods.title: How to find the median
methods.example: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try the problem yourself on paper first, then check every step against mine.
steps: Put all the values in order from smallest to largest.
If there is an odd number of values, the median is the middle one.
If there is an even number, the median is halfway between the two middle values (add them and divide by 2).

methods.title: How to find the mean from a frequency table
methods.example: Classify each as categorical, discrete numerical, or continuous numerical: (a) number of pets, (b) type of transport to school, (c) time to run 100 m, (d) shoe size.
steps: Multiply each value by its frequency.
Add up all those products to get the grand total.
Add up the frequencies to get how many values there are.
Divide the grand total by the number of values.

methods.title: How to choose the best average
methods.example: Find the mean, median and mode of: 3, 7, 5, 9, 5, 8, 6, 5, 4.
steps: Use the mean for even data with no extreme values.
Use the median when there is an outlier.
Use the mode for categorical data or the most common value.

methods.title: The range and consistency
methods.example: Two classes have the same mean of 85. Class A scored 78, 82, 85, 88, 92; Class B scored 65, 70, 85, 95, 100. Which class was more consistent?
steps: Solution: Range of A = 92 − 78 = 14.
Range of B = 100 − 65 = 35.
A smaller range means less variation, so Class A was more consistent, even though both classes have the same mean.
This shows why the range matters as well as the average.

methods.title: Mean or median?
methods.example: Two classes have the same mean of 85. Class A scored 78, 82, 85, 88, 92; Class B scored 65, 70, 85, 95, 100. Which class was more consistent?
steps: Solution: Range of A = 92 − 78 = 14.
Range of B = 100 − 65 = 35.
A smaller range means less variation, so Class A was more consistent, even though both classes have the same mean.
This shows why the range matters as well as the average.

methods.title: Pie chart angles
methods.example: 40 students named a favourite sport: Football 20, Basketball 8, Volleyball 6, Swimming 6. Find the angle for each slice of a pie chart.
steps: Solution: Angle = (frequency ÷ total) × 360°.
Basketball = 8 ÷ 40 × 360° = 72°.
Volleyball = 6 ÷ 40 × 360° = 54°.

workedExamples.title: Statistical or not?
workedExamples.prompt: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try the problem yourself on paper first, then check every step against mine.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Statistical or not? Decide which of these is a statistical question: (a) “How tall is Ibrahim?” (b) “How tall are the students in Year 7?” Solution: Question (a) has a single fixed answer – one measurement – so it is not statistical. Question (b) expects a variety of heights and can only be answered by collecting data from many students, so (b) is the statistical question.

workedExamples.title: Classify the data
workedExamples.prompt: Classify each as categorical, discrete numerical, or continuous numerical: (a) number of pets, (b) type of transport to school, (c) time to run 100 m, (d) shoe size.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) You count pets in whole numbers, so discrete numerical. (b) Walk, bus, car… these are groups, so categorical. (c) Time is measured and can be any value like 14.7 s, so continuous numerical. (d) Shoe sizes come in fixed steps you count, so discrete numerical.

workedExamples.title: Find the mean, median and mode
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median and mode of: 3, 7, 5, 9, 5, 8, 6, 5, 4.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add them: 3 + 7 + 5 + 9 + 5 + 8 + 6 + 5 + 4 = 52. There are 9 values, so the mean = 52 ÷ 9 ≈ 5.8. Order the data: 3, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. The middle (5th) value is the median = 5. The value 5 appears three times, more than any other, so the mode = 5.

workedExamples.title: The range and consistency
workedExamples.prompt: Two classes have the same mean of 85. Class A scored 78, 82, 85, 88, 92; Class B scored 65, 70, 85, 95, 100. Which class was more consistent?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Range of A = 92 − 78 = 14. Range of B = 100 − 65 = 35. A smaller range means less variation, so Class A was more consistent, even though both classes have the same mean. This shows why the range matters as well as the average. Worked Example 5: Mean or median? A shop’s daily customers for a week were 45, 52, 48, 150, 49, 51, 47. Should the owner use the mean or the median to describe a typical day? Solution: The value 150 is an outlier. The mean is 442 ÷ 7 ≈ 63.1, which is higher than six of the seven…

workedExamples.title: Mean or median?
workedExamples.prompt: Two classes have the same mean of 85. Class A scored 78, 82, 85, 88, 92; Class B scored 65, 70, 85, 95, 100. Which class was more consistent?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Range of A = 92 − 78 = 14. Range of B = 100 − 65 = 35. A smaller range means less variation, so Class A was more consistent, even though both classes have the same mean. This shows why the range matters as well as the average. Worked Example 5: Mean or median? A shop’s daily customers for a week were 45, 52, 48, 150, 49, 51, 47. Should the owner use the mean or the median to describe a typical day? Solution: The value 150 is an outlier. The mean is 442 ÷ 7 ≈ 63.1, which is higher than six of the seven…

workedExamples.title: Pie chart angles
workedExamples.prompt: 40 students named a favourite sport: Football 20, Basketball 8, Volleyball 6, Swimming 6. Find the angle for each slice of a pie chart.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angle = (frequency ÷ total) × 360°. Football = 20 ÷ 40 × 360° = 180°. Basketball = 8 ÷ 40 × 360° = 72°. Volleyball = 6 ÷ 40 × 360° = 54°. Swimming = 6 ÷ 40 × 360° = 54°. Check: 180 + 72 + 54 + 54 = 360°. The angles add to a full circle, so it is correct.

workedExamples.title: Mean from a frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: The number of goals scored in 10 matches: 0 goals in 2 matches, 1 goal in 4 matches, 2 goals in 3 matches, 3 goals in 1 match. Find the mean.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply each value by its frequency: (0×2) + (1×4) + (2×3) + (3×1) = 0 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 13 goals. The number of matches is 2 + 4 + 3 + 1 = 10. So the mean = 13 ÷ 10 = 1.3 goals per match.

workedExamples.title: Read a two-way table
workedExamples.prompt: Using the two-way table from Concept 4, how many students in total chose Science, and how many girls chose English?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the Science column total: 15 students chose Science. Find where the Girls row meets the English column: 5 girls chose English. Reading a two-way table is just finding where a row meets a column.

activities.title: Your Own Household Survey
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and family members to ask.
steps: Choose a statistical question, such as “How many cups of tea does each person drink in a day?”
Collect the data from at least six people and record it in a tally chart.
Turn your tally into a frequency table.
Work out the mean, median, mode and range of your data.
Write one honest sentence saying what a typical answer was and how much the answers varied.

activities.title: The Averages Card Game
activities.materials: number cards or paper slips numbered 1 to 20.
steps: Shuffle and deal five cards to yourself.
Find the mean, median, mode (if any) and range of your five numbers.
Deal five to a partner and compare: whose set has the bigger mean? whose has the bigger range?
Play several rounds and keep score of who wins each measure.
This game makes calculating averages fast and automatic.

activities.title: Market Price Investigation
activities.materials: the prices of one item (for example, a mango) from several stalls or shops.
steps: Collect the price of the same item from at least five different sellers.
Record the prices and find the mean price and the range.
The range tells you how much prices vary between sellers – is it large or small?
Write a short conclusion: where is the best value, and how spread out are the prices?

activities.title: Spot the Misleading Graph
activities.materials: newspapers, adverts, or packaging that show charts.
steps: Find any bar chart or graph in an advert or newspaper.
Check the vertical axis – does it start at zero, or somewhere higher?
Decide whether the graph makes a difference look bigger than it really is.
Redraw it fairly with the axis starting at zero and compare the two pictures.

activities.title: Pie Chart from Real Data (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and (if you have one) a protractor; a bowl of mixed items such as dates, stones and beans.
steps: Sort your items into groups and count how many are in each group.
Find the total, then work out each slice angle: (group ÷ total) × 360°.
Check your angles add up to 360°.
Draw the pie chart and label each slice. Explain which group is the largest.

activities.title: Consistency Challenge (Investigation)
activities.materials: a small ball or scrunched paper and a target, or a way to time an activity.
steps: Throw at the target ten times and record how many hits you get in sets of, say, five throws – or time yourself doing a task ten times.
Calculate the mean and the range of your results.
Practise, then repeat. Did your mean improve? Did your range get smaller (more consistent)?
Write about which changed more: your average performance or your consistency.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand averages best? When did the range tell you something the average could not? Where did you spot bias or a misleading graph? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The golden rule of every chart is: label everything. A chart without a title and axis labels is a puzzle no one can read. Choosing the wrong chart type – such as a pie chart for data that is not parts of a whole – confuses your reader instead of helping them.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Be careful about correlation and causation. Two things can rise together without one causing the other. In hot weather, both ice-cream sales and swimming accidents go up – they are correlated – but ice cream does not cause the accidents; the hot weather affects both. The rule to remember is: correlation does not prove causation. Just because two sets of numbers move together does not mean one makes the other happen.

terms: A smaller part of the population
Unfairness that makes results lean the wrong way
How many times a value appears
A value far larger or smaller than the rest
Two things changing together (not always cause)
commonMistakes: Dividing by the wrong number for the mean
Divide by how many values, not the biggest
Letting an outlier spoil the mean
Use the median when there is an outlier
Check the scale before believing it

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Maryam counts the dates each family member eats at iftar: 6, 8, 5, 9, 7, 6, 8. Find the mean, median, mode and range, and describe what the range tells you.
realProblems.answer: 1) Total = 49, mean = 7; ordered 5,6,6,7,8,8,9, median = 7, modes = 6 and 8, range = 9 − 5 = 4 – a small range, so the amounts are fairly consistent.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Total = 49, mean = 7; ordered 5,6,6,7,8,8,9, median = 7, modes = 6 and 8, range = 9 − 5 = 4 – a small range, so the amounts are fairly consistent.

realProblems.prompt: Two football teams from Mogadishu and Kismaayo each played 5 matches. Mogadishu scored 2, 2, 3, 2, 1 (mean 2). Kismaayo scored 0, 4, 1, 5, 0 (mean 2). Compare the teams using the range and say which is more consistent.
realProblems.answer: Mogadishu range = 3 − 1 = 2; Kismaayo range = 5 − 0 = 5; same mean, but Mogadishu has the smaller range so it is more consistent.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mogadishu range = 3 − 1 = 2; Kismaayo range = 5 − 0 = 5; same mean, but Mogadishu has the smaller range so it is more consistent.

realProblems.prompt: A health worker in Garissa surveys 200 families about clean-water access, but only visits homes near the town centre. Explain the sampling problem and how to improve it.
realProblems.answer: Only visiting central homes is a biased, unrepresentative sample; she should sample families randomly from all areas, near and far.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Only visiting central homes is a biased, unrepresentative sample; she should sample families randomly from all areas, near and far.

realProblems.prompt: A newspaper headline says “Sales doubled!” but the bar for the new figure is drawn five times taller than the old one. Explain how the graph could be misleading and what a fair graph would show.
realProblems.answer: Doubling should make the bar twice as tall, not five times; a fair graph starts the axis at zero and uses a consistent scale.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Doubling should make the bar twice as tall, not five times; a fair graph starts the axis at zero and uses a consistent scale.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In a two-way table, 40 students were surveyed. 22 were boys, of whom 15 like football; 12 of the girls like football. Complete the missing totals and state how many students in total like football.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: ÷ 2 = 24, which matches the typical values, so the median is better.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Sagal claims, “The team is inconsistent because its mean score is low.” Explain why the mean alone cannot show consistency, and name the measure that can.
A shop’s weekly customers had a mean of 50 over 7 days and a total you must find. If six of the days were 48, 52, 47, 51, 49 and 53, find the seventh day’s figure.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The mean shows the typical score, not how spread out the scores are; the range measures consistency.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Charts and Graphs.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A good chart lets the eye see a pattern instantly. The skill is choosing the right chart for the type of data. Here are the ones you need in Year 7. Bar charts display categorical data using bars of equal width, with a gap between each bar because the categories are separate. The height of each bar shows its frequency. Always give the chart a title and label both axes. Bar charts are perfect for comparing groups, such as favourite sports or sales by product. A pictogram does the same job using pictures, where one…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Averages and Range.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An average is a single value that stands for a whole set of data – a “typical” value. There are three different averages, and a good statistician knows all three and when to use each. There is also the range, which measures how spread out the data is. The mean is what most people call “the average.” To find it, add up all the values, then divide by how many values there are. Suppose a Kismaayo football team scored these goals in 9 matches: 2, 0, 3, 1, 2, 4, 2, 1, 3. The total is 2 + 0 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 3 =…

selfAssessment: I can tell the difference between a statistical question and a non-statistical question, and plan an investigation to answer one.
I can classify data as categorical or numerical, and numerical data as discrete or continuous.
I can choose a sensible way to collect data, understand population and sample, and recognise bias.
I can organise raw data into frequency tables, including grouped and two-way tables.
I can draw and read bar charts, pictograms, pie charts, line graphs and stem-and-leaf diagrams.
I can calculate the three averages – mean, median and mode – and the range, and know when each average is most useful.
I can interpret data, compare two sets of data, spot misleading graphs, and write valid conclusions.
